coredata.dc:title: A DFT and MD study of reactive, H2 adsorption and optoelectronic properties of graphane nanoparticles – An influence of boron doping
coredata.dc:creator: Rikalo, Aleksandar
Nikolić, Marina
Alanov, Marija
Vuković, Aleksandar
Armaković, Sanja J.
Armaković, Stevan
coredata.dc:description:
Structural, reactive, H2 adsorption and charge transfer properties of graphane nanoparticles modified with boron atom have been investigated using the combination of density functional theory (DFT) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Studies of reactivity encompassed calculations of molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) and average local ionization energy (ALIE) descriptors. It has been demonstrated by these quantum-molecular descriptors that reactivity of studied systems significantly increases in the near vicinity of boron dopants. Adsorption properties of studied graphane and its derivative nanoparticles have been performed towards the hydrogen molecules, in order to assess the potential of studied structures in the area of clean energy sources. Results indicate that introduction of boron atoms greatly improves the H2 binding energies. Symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) calculations enabled us to decompose the interaction energy to physical contributions and to better understand the influence of boron atoms to the H2 adsorption properties. Charge transfer rates and charge mobilities have been calculated according to Marcus semi-empiric approach. Optoelectronic properties of studied nanostructures have been compared to that of pentacene molecule, in order to further draw attention to their potential for practical applications in materials science area. The influence of size of the graphane nanoparticles was also taken into account and calculations show that both reorganization energies and
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter decrease with the increase in size. In case of the largest pristine graphane nanoparticle considered in this work, the electron reorganization energy is lower than in case of the pentacene, while
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter has very low value of just 0.05 eV.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphane nanoparticles
Reactivity
H2 adsorption
Optoelectronics
DFT
MD

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optoelectronic properties of boron substituted GN
Optoelectronic properties of graphane nanoparticles vs. pentacene
The effects of graphane nanoparticle size and boron doping concentration to reactivity and optoelectronics

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Serbia
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministarstvo Prosvete, Nauke i Tehnološkog Razvoja

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was initiated by the CONNECT conference, that was organized in Neum (Bosnia and Herzegovina) in August 2017. CONNECT aims to improve scientific and other collaboration between the students and young researchers of the Western Balkans, and is organized thanks to the support received by the Deutscher Akademischer Austauschdienst (DAAD, eng. German Academic Exchange Service). SA expresses his gratitude also to the project supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Serbia, grant III41017.

This work was initiated by the CONNECT conference, that was organized in Neum (Bosnia and Herzegovina) in August 2017. CONNECT aims to improve scientific and other collaboration between the students and young researchers of the Western Balkans, and is organized thanks to the support received by the Deutscher Akademischer Austauschdienst (DAAD, eng. German Academic Exchange Service) . SA expresses his gratitude also to the project supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Serbia , grant III41017 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: MEP and ALIE surfaces of GN and their boron-substituted derivatives.

H2 molecules adsorbed by GN and boron substituted derivatives: a) pristine GN + perpendicularly oriented H2, b) GNB1 + perpendicularly oriented H2, c) GNB1 + parallel oriented H2, d) GNB2 + parallel oriented H2 and e) GNB3 + perpendicularly oriented H2.
Adsorption of H2 by coronene a) CN + H2 perpendicular and b) CN + H2 parallel.
Two larger models of graphane nanoparticles a) GNCL1 and b) GNCL2.
Boron derivatives of larger models of graphane nanoparticles.
Optimized geometries of structures used for the investigation of influence of boron concentration.
H2 binding energies of studied GN and its derivatives.
SAPT components and H2 binding energies of GN and CN.

Reorganization energies and
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter for studied structures.
Charge transfer rates and reorganization energies for studied structures.
Comparison of optoelectronic properties of GN and pentacene.

Δ E

parameter of larger nanoparticles.
Maximal and minimal values of MEP and ALIE, in [kcal/mol], of graphane nanoparticles with different concentrations of boron atoms.
Optoelectronic properties of graphane nanoparticles with different number of boron atoms.

head.ce:title: A DFT and MD study of reactive, H2 adsorption and optoelectronic properties of graphane nanoparticles – An influence of boron doping

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Novi Sad, Faculty of Sciences, Department of Chemistry, Biochemistry and Environmental Protection, Trg Dositeja Obradovica 3, 21000, Novi Sad, Serbia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Structural, reactive, H2 adsorption and charge transfer properties of graphane nanoparticles modified with boron atom have been investigated using the combination of density functional theory (DFT) calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations. Studies of reactivity encompassed calculations of molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) and average local ionization energy (ALIE) descriptors. It has been demonstrated by these quantum-molecular descriptors that reactivity of studied systems significantly increases in the near vicinity of boron dopants. Adsorption properties of studied graphane and its derivative nanoparticles have been performed towards the hydrogen molecules, in order to assess the potential of studied structures in the area of clean energy sources. Results indicate that introduction of boron atoms greatly improves the H2 binding energies. Symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) calculations enabled us to decompose the interaction energy to physical contributions and to better understand the influence of boron atoms to the H2 adsorption properties. Charge transfer rates and charge mobilities have been calculated according to Marcus semi-empiric approach. Optoelectronic properties of studied nanostructures have been compared to that of pentacene molecule, in order to further draw attention to their potential for practical applications in materials science area. The influence of size of the graphane nanoparticles was also taken into account and calculations show that both reorganization energies and
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter decrease with the increase in size. In case of the largest pristine graphane nanoparticle considered in this work, the electron reorganization energy is lower than in case of the pentacene, while
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter has very low value of just 0.05 eV.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene certainly belongs to a group of the most frequently studied nanostructures, from both experimental and theoretical aspects [1–6]. Just one of the reasons to which graphene owe its popularity is its chemically active surface which is able to host different elements and functional groups. Chemical reactivity of graphene enables attaching of hydrogen atoms to both of its sides, leading to the structure known as graphane. Graphane is also outstanding material which was experimentally realized by Elias et al. [7] in 2009. Two years before experimental conformation of graphane, it was predicted by Sofo et al. [8] and Lu et al. [9].

The model of graphane considers that all carbon atoms have covalent bonds to hydrogen atoms and in the same time this can be fulfilled only if graphene's carbon atoms change their hybridization from sp 2 to sp 3. This leads to the so called buckled structure of graphane, in comparison with the flat structure of graphene. According to reported density functional theory (DFT) calculations, graphane belongs to a group of wide band gap semiconductors [7,10,11]. According to reports, graphene was transformed to graphane by its exposure to hydrogen plasma for the duration of several hours [7]. The study of Savchenko [12] reports the usage of hydrogen plasma for reactions with graphene in order to obtain graphane, however such approach couldn't control the degree of hydrogenation [13]. The new route for preparation graphane was reported by Wang et al. [14] and is based on the plasma-enhanced chemical vapor deposition.

Similarly as graphene, graphane has great potential for practical applications in different research areas. For example, graphane has been considered by Nechae et al. [15] in context of challenges related to hydrogen storage. In these regards modifications of graphane with alkali metals for the improvement binding energies and storage capacities have been considered in studies by Antipina et al. [16] and Hussain et al. [17].

Graphane's potential for applications in organic electronics have been reported in works by Gharekhanlou et al. [18,19]. On the other side, ideal graphane structures are not expected to exhibit magnetic properties, however in works by Sahin et al. [20] and Berashevich and Chakraborty [21] it has been determined that magnetism occurs at defect sites, i.e. the ones which are missing the hydrogen atoms. In connection with magnetism, an interesting study was performed by Wang et al. in which the influence of doping of graphane with different atoms has been studied. By spin polarized calculations it was determined that substitution of graphane sheets with boron and aluminum atoms induces the magnetic properties of these materials.

Graphane systems are considered to be potentially applied for the purpose of hydrogen storage. Hussain et al. [17] computationally investigated the potential of graphane modified with calcium atoms for adsorption of 2–10 molecules of hydrogen and obtained competitive binding energies. Lithium atoms as a tool for improvement of adsorption properties of graphane towards the hydrogen molecules was also studied in another studies by Hussain et al. [22,23]. Potential for hydrogen adsorption by graphane and its derivatives has been also in focus of many studies [24,25].

In general, literature survey reveals the great potential of graphane for practical applications in a variety of areas. The aim of this work was to assess fundamental properties of graphane nanoparticles and to investigate how modifications with boron atoms influence their structural, reactive, H2 adsorption and optoelectronic properties. For these purposes a combination of DFT calculations and molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have been applied, as these theoretical approaches have proven their applicability for understanding of fundamental properties at atomic level.

All calculations in this work have been performed with programs incorporated in Schrödinger Materials Science Suites 2018-2 (SMSS). DFT calculations have been performed with Jaguar program [26–28], MD simulations have been performed with Desmond program [29–31], while visualization of results and preparation of input files have been performed with Maestro program [32]. DFT calculations have been performed with B3LYP exchange-correlation functional [33], along with 6-31G(d,p) basis set. Geometrical optimizations have been performed for all structures and true ground states were verified by vibrational analysis at the same level of theory, which produced only positive frequencies.

MD simulations have been performed with OPLS3e force field [29,34–36]. The simulation time was set to 10 ns, while the cut off radius was set to 10 Å. Other parameters for MD simulation encompassed temperature of 300 K and pressure of 1.0325 bar. MD systems consisted of 32 molecules of each of structure studied in this work, as obtained by disordered structure tool implemented in SMSS.

Systems for investigations of adsorption properties consisted of graphane derivative nanoparticle (GN) and hydrogen molecule placed above it. Counterpoise correction [37] has been taken into account in order to obtain H2 binding energies according to the following equation:
(1)
E
b
=
E
tot
( GN +
H
2
) + E
(
GN
)
+ E
(
H
2
)
+ E
(
BSSE
)
.

In equation (1),
E
tot
(
GN +
H
2
)
marks the total ground state energy of the system consisting of GN + H2 molecule,
E
(
GN
)
stands for the total ground state energy of the GN.
E
(
H
2
)
stands for the total ground state energy of the hydrogen molecule, while
E
(
BSSE
)
stands for the basis superposition error (BSSE). For investigation of H2 adsorption properties of graphane nanoparticles we have employed the dispersion corrected B3LYP functional, namely the B3LYP-D3 [38], which presents an adequate choice when it comes to the noncovalent interactions between molecules. Again, the 6-31G(d,p) basis set was applied and all of the obtained ground state geometries were also checked by the vibrational analysis.

Equation which was used for the calculations of charge transfer rates of holes and electrons according to Marcus theory was the following [39,40]:
(2)
k
CT
i
=
2
π
ℏ
e
− L
4
k
T
(
H
a b
)
2
4
π
λ
i
k
T
,
i
represents holes (+) or electrons (−). Reorganization energies are represented by
λ
i
, while the
H
a b
is charge transfer integral. Charge transfer integrals are also known as charge coupling or coupling matrix element.
k
represents the Boltzmann constant, while
T
is the temperature. The unit of
k
C T
i
is
s
− 1
.

A combination of B3LYP functional and MIDIX basis set has been employed for calculations of reorganization energies. The relatively small MIDIX basis set provides results similar to 6-31G(d) basis set, but it is much more computationally affordable. This combination has been also used in our recent studies [41–44] and obtained reliable results. Reorganization energies were calculated according to the equations:
(3)
λ
1
=
E
0
(
G
*
)
−
E
0
(
G
0
)
,
(4)
λ
2
=
E
*
(
G
0
)
−
E
*
(
G
*
)
,
(5)
λ
i
=
λ
1
+
λ
2
,

In equation (3)
E
0
(
G
0
)
represents the ground state energy of the neutral state, while
E
0
(
G
*
)
is the energy of the neutral molecule at the optimal ionic geometry. In equation (4)
E
*
(
G
*
)
denotes the ground state energy of the ionic states, while
E
*
(
G
0
)
is the energy of the charged state at the optimal geometry of the neutral molecule.

Calculations based on symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) approach were performed by PSI4 modeling program [45,46]. Concretely, the SAPT0 [47,48] approach was used along with the jun-cc-pVDZ basis set.

As it has been frequently emphasized in scientific literature, graphane is hydrogenated graphene structure. In order for carbon-hydrogen bonds to occur within such 2D nanostructure, a hybridization of the carbon atoms of graphene layer has to be changed from sp 2 to sp 3 type. This leads to the so called buckled structure of the graphane, contrary to the completely flat structure characteristic for graphene. The buckled structure of GN and GNB derivatives has been reproduced by geometrical optimization and the optimized structures have been presented in Fig. 1 .

As expected the boron atoms influenced the structure of GN by “flatting” the structure and producing the changes in charge distribution, which will be commented somewhat later. To study the influence of boron atoms to the structure of GN and its derivatives, we have compared the carbon–carbon (C–C) and carbon–boron (C–B) bond lengths. Namely, the C–C bond length in pristine GN has been calculated to be 1.54 Å, which is in agreement with some previous studies, for example in review by Zhou et al. [13]. The presence of boron atoms increased the bond lengths to 1.56 Å in case of the GN1 derivative, i.e. 1.57 and 1.58 Å in case of the remaining derivatives. These increase of the C–B bond lengths increase of the graphane nanoparticles is in agreement with the results obtained by the Mapasha et al. [49], who investigated selected properties of periodic boron-substituted graphane structures.

The influence of boron doping to reactive properties of GN has been addressed using the two quantum-molecular descriptors of fundamental importance – MEP and ALIE. The fundamentality of these two quantities is reflected through fact that they depend on the electronic density, which is experimentally measurable quantity. For a significant amount of time, MEP is considered to be a valuable tool for the identification of the molecular sites sensitive to interactions with other molecular species via electrostatic interactions [50–52].

While the concept of MEP has been applied with great efficiency for prediction of reactivity of various organic molecules, when it comes to the prediction of sensitivity towards the electrophilic attacks, it is better to use the ALIE descriptor. This descriptor is defined as an energy that has to be invested in order to remove an electron from the certain point of the molecule. The most important molecular sites indicated by this descriptors are the ones for which the lowest values of ALIE are calculated. At these positions the electrons are least tightly bounded and the lowest energy is required in order to remove them. These sites are also most sensitive for the reactions with electrophiles or radicals. To obtain full picture on the local reactivity of GN and their derivatives obtained by boron substitution we have utilized both descriptors. MEP and ALIE values have been calculated and their values have been mapped to the electron density surface, Fig. 2 .

MEP and ALIE surfaces of studied structures indicate interesting and important changes of these descriptors as a consequence of modifications with boron atoms. The first specific change that occurred as a consequence of presence of boron atoms is the high increase of maximal MEP values, from 4.50 kcal/mol in case of the pristine GN to 21.94 kcal/mol in case of the GNB1 derivative. All maximal MEP values of boron modified GN nanoparticles are higher than 20 kcal/mol. GNB1 derivative also has the lowest MEP value, which designates this derivative as the most reactive in terms of electrostatic interactions. On the other side, the lowest MEP values of pristine GN and its boron modified derivatives are having values between 3 and 4 kcal/mol.

From the aspect of ALIE descriptor, GNB1 derivative again was the most important one. Namely, in all other cases maximal and minimal ALIE values remained unchanged, while in case of the GNB1 derivative the minimal ALIE value lowered for 13 kcal/mol. These results indicate that substitution of GN with boron atoms might significantly improve the reactive properties of GN, including sensitivities towards both electrostatic interactions and electrophilic attacks.

The consequences of the boron substitutions to GN have been also analyzed from the aspect of adsorption properties towards the hydrogen molecules. We will firstly refer to the H2 binding energies, which is one of the most important parameters when it comes to the efficient adsorption. Adsorption modes, together with the formed noncovalent interactions have been visualized in Fig. 3 , while H2 binding energies have been summarized in Table 1 .

All adsorption calculations have been setup with H2 placed both perpendicularly and parallel above the GN and its derivatives. Additionally, in cases of boron-substituted GN derivatives, H2 molecules have been placed above the central part of nanoparticle and above the boron atom, again in both orientations. Such approach ensured that all possible adsorption modes are considered.

As indicated in Fig. 3, pristine GN according to our calculations binds only perpendicularly oriented H2 with H2 binding energy of −0.82 kcal/mol. The same orientation, the same location and practically the same H2 binding energy have been obtained in cases of GNB1 and GNB2 derivatives. Additionally, GNB1 derivative adsorbs H2 molecule in parallel orientation, with significantly higher binding energy of −1.41 kcal/mol. The highest H2 binding energy of −1.88 kcal/mol has been calculated in case of the GNB3 derivative, which adsorbs H2 molecule only in parallel orientation. The results of this chapter indicate that substitution of GN with boron atoms significantly improves the adsorption properties towards H2 molecules, which may be of particular importance from the aspects of environmental protection and development of sensing devices based on these nanoparticles. The binding energies calculated in this study are higher than the ones obtained for metal-atom-modified graphane. For example, Hussain and coworkers [17] have reported H2 binding energies for 2–10 hydrogen molecules adsorbed by calcium doped (Ca-doped) graphane. For two and three hydrogen molecules adsorbed by Ca-doped graphane they have reported binding energies of 0.23 and 1.61 kcal/mol, using the periodic DFT calculations within generalized gradient approximation (GGA) and by taking into account the corrections regarding the van der Waals interactions. Antipina et al. [16] have also investigated adsorption properties of graphane modified with alkali metal atoms. In cases of the adsorption of one hydrogen molecule, they have reported the H2 binding energies of 0.92 kcal/mol, 1.38 kcal/mol and 1.38 kcal/mol for potassium, lithium and sodium modified graphane, respectively.

Fig. 3 also contains information about the formed noncovalent interactions between graphane nanoparticles and hydrogen molecules. Namely, the noncovalent interactions are indicated by red dotted lines. It can be seen in Fig. 3 that the highest number of noncovalent interactions, a total of 6, formed precisely in case of the GNB3 derivative, for which the highest H2 binding energy has been obtained. Higher H2 binding energies have been obtained for parallel orientation of H2 molecules, an orientation which enables formation of higher number of noncovalent interactions [53].

Better H2 binding energies as a consequence of doping with boron atom have been also explained by the analysis of charge polarization. Namely, pristine GN considered in our work practically has no charge polarization, due to its symmetry, i.e. its dipole moment is equal to zero. However, the introduction of boron atom induced the charge polarization and, consequently, the dipole moment. The dipole moment of GN derivatives with boron atoms induced the dipole moment in H2 molecules and this lead to the improved electrostatic interactions and binding energies between these molecules.

However, binding energies provide only a partial insight into the reactivity between studied molecules. In order to gain additional insights and to understand more deeply the influence of boron dopants, when it comes to the H2 adsorption, in this work we have also performed a calculations based on the symmetry-adapted perturbation theory (SAPT) approach (particularly the SAPT0 variant). Thanks to the SAPT approach, noncovalent interaction between molecules can be decomposed into the physical contributions such as: electrostatic (EL), exchange (i), induction (I) and dispersion (D) contributions. By obtaining all of these components for the systems visualized in Fig. 3, we were able to determine which of the components significantly change and principally dictate the H2 binding strength. SAPT results have been summarized in Table 2 .

Results in Table 2 indicate that the components of interaction energy between graphane nanoparticles and H2 significantly change as a consequence of the introduction of boron atom. It can be also seen that interaction energy (T SAPT0) fully follows the trend of calculated H2 binding energies. Analyzing Fig. 3 it is clear that the H2 binding energy greatly improves if the adsorbed H2 molecule is located in the near vicinity of boron dopant (cases visualized in Fig. 3c and e). In other cases, boron dopant has no influence to the H2 binding energies. Therefore, it is particularly interesting to compare the SAPT interaction energy components of system presented in Fig. 3a (system GN + H2) with systems presented in Fig. 3c (GNB1 + H2 parallel) and Fig. 3e (GNB3 + H2).

According to the results presented in Table 2, all SAPT components of systems GNB1 + H2 parallel and GNB3 + H2 increase their magnitudes, in comparison with the system GN + H2. Firstly, the magnitude of EX component, responsible for the Coulombic multipole-multipole interactions and the overlap between monomeric charge distributions, increases for more than three times in case of the GNB1 + H2 parallel system and for almost four times in case of the GNB3 + H2. The I component, responsible for the polarization coming from the response of monomers to each other's electric field and which is also related to the charge transfer between monomers, doubles its magnitude in cases of systems denoted as GNB1 + H2 parallel and GNB3 + H2. The D component, describing the correlation between electrons of monomers, also increases its magnitude for 30% in case of the GNB1 + H2 parallel system and for more than two times in case of the GNB3+H2 system. The magnitudes of attracting SAPT components are the highest in case of the GNB3+H2 system, which is also characterized by the highest H2 binding energy. In this particular case, the boron doping caused the highest increase of the EX component, followed by the increases of D and I components.

Since the coronene is frequently used model for modeling of graphene properties and since in this paper we used a hydrogenated coronene model to investigate properties of graphane, it is possible to conclude what are the roles of hydrogen atoms of graphane. For these purposes we have also calculated H2 binding energies in case of coronene (CN), and the resulting systems were also used for SAPT calculations. Systems of coronene + H2 where H2 is oriented perpendicular and parallel to coronene are denoted as CN + H2 perpendicular and CN + H2 parallel. The obtained results are summarized in Table 3 , while resulting geometries of H2 adsorption by CN are provided in Fig. 4 .

Results presented in Table 3 indicate that there is a relatively significant difference in H2 adsorption between GN and CN, while Fig. 4 indicates that CN can bind H2 molecule in both (approximately) perpendicular and parallel orientations. Analysis of SAPT decomposition of interaction energy indicates that two contributions greatly depend on the hydrogen atoms – EL and D. These two components are significantly higher in case when hydrogen atoms are absent, i.e. when we deal with the CN model in comparison to GN model. It also appears that hydrogen atoms influence the D component to higher extent. I component is practically the same, when it comes to the H2 adsorption by GN and CN, which is to be expected since both GN and CN don't have dipole moments due to the symmetry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optoelectronic properties of boron substituted GN
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to be considered as a candidate for applications in organic electronics, observed molecular structure has to be characterized by high charge mobilities and to be suitable for charge injection [54]. Among many candidates, the most perspective ones can be identified thanks to the DFT calculations. For these purposes charge hooping rates,
k
CT
i
, can be calculated in order to evaluate charge transport properties of candidates, by using equation (2). In this work we have firstly calculated some of the most important optoelectronic properties – reorganization energies and energy difference between the first excited singlet and triplet states,
ΔE
(
S 1 − T 1
)
. The
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter is very important quantity due to the fact that it indicates the possibility of some structure to exhibit a thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) mechanism. The importance of TADF mechanism lies in the fact that it allows the production of OLED materials with great efficiency but without the presence of the precious metal atoms [55–57]. In these regards it is important to note that
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter should be lower than ca. 0.37 eV, since these values allow effective thermal population of S1 state [58,59]. Reorganization energies and
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter of studied derivatives in this work have been summarized in Table 4 .

Reorganization energy of electrons in case of the pristine GN is having a low and competitive value of 0.17 eV. On the other side the reorganization energy of holes is significantly higher. Introduction of boron atoms to GN structure significantly increases the reorganization energy of electrons, while reorganization energies of holes remained practically the same. Certainly very interesting results have been obtained for the
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter. Namely, in case of the pristine GN, the
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter is having the value of just 0.07 eV, which is very promising value, much lower than the desired threshold of ~0.37 eV. Same as in the case of reorganization energies of electrons,
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter also increased significantly as a consequence of boron substitution, but the value of GNB3 derivative is 0.41 eV, which is very close to the desired threshold value. These results indicate very promising future of graphane nanoparticles when it comes to the TADF mechanism and development of new OLED devices.

Further we have used information on reorganization energies and calculated charge transfer rates according to equation (2). The H ab parameter has been calculated by the dimer splitting approximation, according to coupling matrix element is approximated as the difference between energies of LUMO and LUMO+1 for the transfer of electrons, i.e. as the difference between energies of HOMO and HOMO+1 for holes. Charge transfer rates have been calculated for dimer structures obtained by MD simulations. Namely, systems consisting of 32 molecules have been simulated performed on the basis of MD in order to obtain relevant dimers of studied structures. For each GN derivative, charge transfer rates have been calculated for half of the total number of pairs (randomly chosen) and then the charge transfer rates have been taken as an average values. Charge transfer rates have been summarized in Table 5 .

The analysis of equation (2) indicates that for high charge transfer rates it is necessary to have minimal reorganization energies and maximal values of charge coupling. In these regards it was expected to obtain the highest charge transfer rates in case of the pristine GN, since reorganization energies of electrons in this case are having very low value of just 0.17 eV. Indeed, the highest transfer rates have been calculated for electrons of GN, with the value of 1.94 × 1014 s−1 (Table 5). Charge transfer rates of holes in case of the GN were calculated to be three orders of magnitude lower, i.e. 2.36 × 1011 s−1. Introduction of boron atoms had one negative and two positive effects to the charge transfer rates. Firstly, while boron atoms decreased the charge transfer rates of electrons, they increased the charge transfer of holes. For example, the charge transfer rates of holes in cases of GNB1 and GNB3 derivatives increased in comparison with pristine GN.

Secondly, the introduction of boron atoms practically eliminated the large difference in magnitudes between hole and electron charge transfer rates. While it is desirable to have as large as possible charge transfer rates, the large difference in magnitudes of these quantities may lead to the recombination of electrons and holes, and therefore the decrease in performances. In these regards it should be noticed that charge transfer rates of holes and electrons are of the same magnitude in case of the GNB3 derivative, while in the same time the charge transfer rates of holes in case of this derivative are significantly higher in comparison with pristine GN.

Aside of the values of reorganization energies, better charge transfer properties of holes in comparison with electrons in cases of GN derivatives with boron atoms can be explained by the values of charge transfer integral. Namely, upon modifications with boron atoms, H ab parameter reduced for electrons and increased for holes. In combination with the values of reorganization energies, this lead to the decrease of charge transfer rates of electrons and increased charge transfer of holes. Also, analysis of energies of molecular orbitals next to the HOMO and LUMO, namely the HOMO-1 and LUMO+1, for monomers (GNB1, GNB2 and GNB3 in comparison with pristine GN) also indicate that LUMO orbitals are more influenced (they are more energetically separated) by the introduction of boron atoms, which goes in favor why holes are having higher charge transfer rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optoelectronic properties of graphane nanoparticles vs. pentacene
ce:section.ce:para:
Certainly the best way to emphasize the potential of structures studied in this work is to compare them with other organic structure that is well-known in the area of organic electronics. In these regards we decided to compare the results of GN and its boron substituted derivatives with those of pentacene, a well-known and frequently studied organic molecule for OLED devices. Firstly, pristine GN has competitive value of electron reorganization energy to that of pentacene (0.13 eV) [44]. In our recently published work [41], for the purpose of comparison of pentacene and curved carbon molecules known as buckybowls, we have calculated charge transfer rates for the pentacene dimer with the highest orbital overlap. Such dimer consists of two pentacene molecules placed one above another in parallel manner, which leads to the maximal charge transfer rates, provided in Table 6 . Besides charge transfer rates, Table 6 also contains the information on
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter, reduction and oxidation potentials, RP and OP respectively.

Results provided in Table 6 indicate that GN has higher electron transfer rates than pentacene, even though they are compared with the maximal transfer rates of pentacene, since in case of the pentacene in our previous paper these have been calculated for dimer configuration with the highest orbital overlap.

The potential for practical applications of graphane nanoparticles are also reflected through
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
values. Namely, this quantity has very high value of 1.41 eV in case of the pentacene, while in case of the pristine GN it has very interesting value of just 0.07 eV, indicating potential for applications on the basis of TADF mechanism. The GNB3 derivative also has
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
value very close to the desired threshold lower than 0.37 eV.

Besides outstanding electron transport properties, pentacene has serious drawbacks related to its poor stability. The main stability drawback of pentacene is the fact that it is easily oxidized in air [60]. In these regards the calculations of oxidation potentials (OP) have been performed in order to compare the GN and pentacene's oxidation sensitivity. The more negative/less positive OP is, the more likely the oxidation is to occur. According to the information provided in Table 6 it can be seen that GN and its derivative have much higher OP, indicating much better stability towards oxidation, in comparison with pentacene.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effects of graphane nanoparticle size and boron doping concentration to reactivity and optoelectronics
ce:section.ce:para: In this work the model of graphane nanoparticle was obtained by hydrogenation of a coronene molecule, which is very frequently used as a model of graphene in order to study its various structural, reactive and adsorption properties. However, the logical question arises; how does the size of considered graphane nanoparticle influence the selected properties. In order to answer this, in this study we have also considered larger models of graphane nanoparticles, presented in Fig. 5 .

The larger models of graphane nanoparticles visualized in Fig. 5 have been named as GNCL1 and GNCL2. The corresponding derivatives made by modifications with boron atom have been presented in Fig. 6 .

After geometrical optimizations all of the structures have been studied for their reactive and optoelectronic properties. H2 adsorption properties haven't been studied for these larger nanoparticles, since it was clearly shown that H2 molecule would bind only above the boron atom, and in those regards very similar results are expected in cases of GNCL1 and GNCL2.
In terms of reactive properties, information about maximal and minimal values of MEP and ALIE descriptors has been summarized in Table 7 .

Results provided in Table 7 are in agreement with the results provided in Fig. 2. Considering pristine graphane nanoparticles, there are no significant differences in the lowest and highest MEP and ALIE values. When it comes to the derivatives with boron atom, it has been established that introduction of boron atom significantly improves the reactivity. Namely, if the introduced boron atom is located within the edges of GN, it produces the highest effect to the values of MEP and ALIE. The magnitudes of minimal and maximal MEP values significantly increase, while the lowest ALIE value significantly decreases, in comparison with the pristine models. Regarding the optoelectronic properties, larger GN models also have promising values, Table 8 .

Results in Table 8 certainly further emphasize the potential of graphane nanoparticles in terms of optoelectronic properties. The values for pristine models are firstly represented and are provided in bold type font. Same as in case of the smaller nanoparticles, reorganization energies of electrons are having highly competitive values. Also, it can be concluded that reorganization of both electrons and holes decrease with the increase in nanoparticle size. The electron reorganization energy in case of the GNCL2 model, the largest one, is having very low value of 0.11 eV, which is lower even than in case of the pentacene molecule. Beside reorganization energies,
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter decreases as well with the increase in size of the graphane nanoparticle. The value of
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter in case of the GNCL2 was calculated to be equal to just 0.05 eV, which is highly encouraging for the possible practical applications of graphane nanoparticles in the devices based on TADF mechanism.

The effects of boron doping concentration have been studied with respect to the GN model. The GN structure was decorated with 2, 4 and 6 boron atoms symmetrically in two ways, with respect to the distance between boron dopants. In that way we obtained two sets of GN derivatives named as nB@GNp, where n (taking values 2, 4 and 6) denotes the number of boron dopants, while p (taking values 1 and 2) denotes the set of GN nanoparticles. In the first set (p = 1), the distance between boron dopants is higher, while in the second set (p = 2) the distance between boron dopants is lower. Geometrically optimized structures that have been used for the investigation of how different boron concentration influences selected properties have been presented in Fig. 7 .

The structures presented in Fig. 7 have been firstly subjected to the calculations of MEP and ALIE descriptors, in order to check how different concentrations of boron doping might influence the reactivity of graphane nanoparticles. In these regards, the maximal and minimal values of MEP and ALIE descriptors have been summarized in Table 9 .

Results in Table 9 demonstrate that the presence of more boron atoms in graphane nanoparticles might be highly beneficial in terms of reactivity. Namely, with the increase of number of boron atoms, the magnitudes of the lowest and the minimal MEP values drastically increase for the GN1 set, while for the GN2 set these values also increase, however, to much lower extent. GN1 set is also characterized by the lower minimal ALIE values, while the GN2 set was practically uninfluenced by the boron atoms in terms of this descriptor.

Last, but not the least, we are referring to the optoelectronic properties of the graphane nanoparticles with different concentrations of the boron atoms. Namely, for the structures presented in Fig. 6, we have also calculated the reorganization energies and the
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter and these results are summarized in Table 10 .

Results presented in Table 10 show that introduction of more boron atoms might be beneficial for graphene nanoparticles and their optoelectronic properties. Speaking of electron reorganization energies, graphane nanoparticles decorated with more boron atoms have better values of this parameter than all the other graphane nanoparticles modified with one boron atom. On the other side, hole reorganization energies are having slightly higher values than derivatives with just one boron atom. The
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter also has competitive values. Although it is not as low as in case of the pristine particles, there are two derivatives decorated with more than one boron atom that are having values significantly lower than the upper border threshold of 0.37 eV. Namely, derivatives 4B@GN2 and 6B@GN2 are having the values of
Δ E
(
S
1
−
T
1
)
parameter equal to 0.29 eV and 0.25 eV, respectively.

Detailed computational investigation of graphane nanoparticles modified with boron atoms was performed by means of DFT calculations and MD simulations. Specifically, the influence of boron atoms to reactive, adsorption and transport properties has been evaluated. DFT calculations firstly showed that reactivity of graphane nanoparticles significantly changes, since maximal and minimal values of MEP and ALIE descriptors changed significantly, especially in case of the graphane nanoparticle model denoted as GN1. For this particular model, magnitudes of MEP descriptor drastically increased, while the minimal ALIE value decreased for more than 13 kcal/mol.

DFT calculations also showed that adsorption towards the H2 molecule, which is of great importance for practical applications in the green energy area, also significantly improve as a consequence of the modifications with boron atoms. Namely, the modifications with boron atoms increased the H2 binding energy from −0.82 kcal/mol to −1.41 kcal/mol and −1.88 kcal/mol. In order to understand changes in binding energies, noncovalent interactions between graphane nanoparticle models and H2 molecule have been studied. It has been shown that number of noncovalent interactions between graphane nanoparticles and H2 molecules increase as a consequence of introduced boron atoms.

The analysis of charge polarization of studied nanoparticles indicated that the introduction of boron atoms induced a dipole moment, which improved the interaction between graphane nanoparticles and H2 molecule on the basis of electrostatic interactions. This was also confirmed by the SAPT calculations, which enabled us to calculate the interaction energy and to decompose it to physical contributions. The analysis of SAPT components showed that the EL component increased to the highest extent.

In order to gain an insight how hydrogen atoms of graphane actually influence the H2 adsorption, we have also studied the H2 adsorption by coronene, a molecule which frequently serves for modeling of graphene. Again by analysis of SAPT components, it was concluded that hydrogen atoms of graphane lower the magnitudes of EL and D components, in comparison to coronene.

By combination of DFT calculations and MD simulations it has been shown that graphane nanoparticles could have superior optoelectronic properties, and that introduction of boron atoms positively influence the charge transport properties between these structures. In these regards, DFT calculations firstly shown that pristine GN model possess very competitive values of reorganization energies, especially in case of the electron reorganization energies. While GN model modified with boron atom has significantly higher electron reorganization energy in comparison with pristine GN model, its hole reorganization energy is practically same as GN. Thanks to the MD simulations, all possible orientations of graphane nanoparticles were obtained. For all of those orientations the charge transfer integral parameter, together with charge transfer rates, has been calculated. In case of pristine GN it has been shown that this structure possesses highly competitive values when it comes to the charge transfer rates. It has been also established that introduction of boron atoms decrease the charge transfer rates of electrons and keeps the charge transfer rates of holes at the same level. Although introduction of boron atoms decreases charge transfer rates of electrons, it still can be considered as a positive effect, since the charge transfer rates of two types of charge carriers are counterbalanced, thanks to which the recombination of charge carriers is prevented.

DFT calculations have shown one more outstanding insight into the optoelectronic properties of studied models. Namely, the
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
value of pristine GN has been calculated to be equal to 0.07 eV. This is great result considering the fact that the upper border level of this parameter is 0.37 eV, in order to be a candidate for applications in organic electronic devices based on TADF mechanism. The
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
value of boron modified GN also has very competitive value of 0.41 eV, which is very close to the desired value of 0.37 eV. In order to demonstrate the potential of structures studied in this work, their optoelectronic properties have been compared to that of pentacene molecule, a well-known molecule in the area of organic electronics.

Study of larger graphane nanoparticles indicated that the reorganization energies and
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter decrease with the increase of size. It was particularly interesting to find that the
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter of the largest considered graphane nanoparticle has a value of just 0.05 eV. In case of the same nanoparticle, the electron reorganization energy is lower than that of pentacene. Finally, the influence of boron concentration was considered and it was concluded that derivatives with more boron atoms are having lower values of electron reorganization energies and
Δ E
(
S 1 − T 1
)
parameter than derivatives modified with one boron atom.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was initiated by the CONNECT conference, that was organized in Neum (Bosnia and Herzegovina) in August 2017. CONNECT aims to improve scientific and other collaboration between the students and young researchers of the Western Balkans, and is organized thanks to the support received by the Deutscher Akademischer Austauschdienst (DAAD, eng. German Academic Exchange Service). SA expresses his gratitude also to the project supported by the Ministry of Education, Science and Technological Development of Serbia, grant III41017.

Wiley Interdiscipl. Rev.: Comput. Mol. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.G. Hohenstein, R.M. Parrish, C.D. Sherrill, J.M. Turney, and H.F. Schaefer III, Large-scale symmetry-adapted perturbation theory computations via density fitting and Laplace transformation techniques: Investigating the fundamental forces of DNA-intercalator interactions. The Journal of chemical physics, 2011. 135(17): p. 174107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Density fitting and Cholesky decomposition approximations in symmetry-adapted perturbation theory: implementation and application to probe the nature of π-π interactions in linear acenes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.G. Hohenstein and C.D. Sherrill, Density fitting and Cholesky decomposition approximations in symmetry-adapted perturbation theory: Implementation and application to probe the nature of π-π interactions in linear acenes. The Journal of Chemical Physics, 2010. 132(18): p. 184111.
